title: Invariant Distances on Legendrian Spaces
speaker: Pierre-Alexandre Arlove
abstract:
I will begin by motivating the study of invariant distances on spaces of Legendrians. I will then discuss two main results:(a) the construction of a new unbounded invariant distance on the universal cover of many Legendrian isotopy classes ;(b) the discreteness of any invariant distance on Legendrian isotopy classes.
In particular, we will see that (a) arises from contact rigidity, whereas (b) follows from contact flexibility.

proof_stages.label: Contact and Legendrian spaces as the arena
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines contact manifolds, contactomorphism groups and their universal covers, proves that point-based invariant distances are trivial by transitivity, and therefore shifts attention to Legendrian isotopy spaces and their universal covers.

proof_stages.label: Known unbounded discriminant distances
proof_stages.summary: After introducing the main model Legendrian families, the speaker recalls Colin--Sandon's unbounded discriminant distance, interpreted as a word metric counting unavoidable embedded Legendrian-isotopy pieces or intersections.

proof_stages.label: Orderability and the earlier positive-contact-loop criterion
proof_stages.summary: The Reeb-flow examples are distilled into two abstract ingredients—positive periodic contact motion and noncontractible positive Legendrian loops—leading to the definition of orderability and the previous theorem that orderability plus a positive contactomorphism loop implies unboundedness.

proof_stages.label: Theorem A and its non-vacuous torus application
proof_stages.summary: The new theorem replaces the positive contactomorphism-loop assumption by a positive Legendrian loop that merely extends to a contactomorphism loop, and the torus-conormal example shows this is a genuine generalization.

proof_stages.label: Order-theoretic machinery for Theorem A
proof_stages.summary: The proof introduces the nonnegative-isotopy preorder, uses orderability to make it a partial order, and proves cofinality lemmas showing that iterates of the extended loop bound arbitrary Legendrian classes above and below.

proof_stages.label: Construction of the unbounded invariant distance
proof_stages.summary: Using the cofinal iterates, the speaker defines the minimal integer needed to order one Legendrian between two loop iterates of another, then checks invariance, nondegeneracy, the triangle inequality, unboundedness, and a comparison with spectral-type distances.

proof_stages.label: Discreteness theorem and local displacement tools
proof_stages.summary: The second main result is stated: any invariant distance on a Legendrian isotopy class has a uniform positive gap; its proof is prepared using the Legendrian neighborhood theorem and a local contactomorphism lemma in the one-jet model to move bounded sets while fixing the zero section.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction proof of the uniform gap
proof_stages.summary: The local support-control corollary bounds the displacement of a fixed Legendrian by twice its distance to any other Legendrian, so a hypothetical sequence of distinct Legendrians converging to it would contradict a compactly supported perturbation that moves it a positive distance.

examples.name: Trivial invariant distance on points
examples.note: The calculation with a distance function on pairs of points uses 2-transitivity of the contactomorphism group to show all nonzero distances would be constant, motivating the move from points to more rigid Legendrian submanifolds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker proves directly that any invariant distance on points is constant by using 2-transitivity and computing d(x,y)=d(x0,y0)=c.

examples.name: Real projective Legendrian RP^n in RP^{2n+1}
examples.note: The projective Legendrian obtained from the small sphere in the odd contact sphere is used to show how Reeb/Hopf-flow iterations create unavoidable intersections, from which the hypotheses of periodic positive motion and noncontractibility/orderability are extracted.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs RP^n as a Legendrian in RP^{2n+1} and then runs the Reeb/Hopf flow on it, tracking repeated intersections and distance growth.

examples.name: Zero section in the periodic one-jet bundle

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes the periodic one-jet bundle zero section and analyzes the Reeb translation flow on it, explaining the return intersections and unbounded discriminant distance.

examples.name: Hypersurface torus conormal in P T^*T^n
examples.note: The projectivized conormal of T^{n-1} inside T^n illustrates that Theorem A is a genuine strengthening, since it has a positive Legendrian loop extendable to a contactomorphism loop but no positive contactomorphism loop.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the torus hypersurface conormal satisfies the new theorem’s hypotheses, identifying its positive Legendrian loop and its extension by q_n-translation.

examples.name: Hofer and Chekanov symplectic distances
examples.note: The Hofer metric on Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms and Chekanov's relative Lagrangian metric are cited as symplectic analogues that contextualize why contact and Legendrian invariant distances are expected to be more discrete.
